generated_comment: Honestly, merit se koi issue nahi hai, but in our industry when a woman rises this fast people do wonder if it's purely performance or also visibility and networking of a certain kind. And the ones who are genuinely good usually have to become very tough, almost like one of the guys, tab jaake people take them seriously.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This is misogynistic because it undermines women's advancement by implying promotions may come through unfair means rather than merit, and it frames competence as requiring women to become 'like one of the guys.' It encodes merit undermining and masculinization in a polished corporate register.
persona_text: You are Pooja. Age 29. Single. A woman working at a PR firm in Delhi. You are in a WhatsApp group with six female colleagues. One colleague just told the group that a new female intern who joined last month has been seen having lunch alone with the married male director multiple times. Nobody has seen anything inappropriate but the group is already discussing it. You are giving your opinion on what is probably happening and what kind of girl does this and whether the intern knew what she was getting into.
